mnemonics.title: I Want My Brother's Love
mnemonics.content: Inputs × Weights + Bias = Output → Activation Function. I(Inputs) W(eights) B(ias) L(ove/activation) helps remember: I × W + B → Activation

mnemonics.title: Silly Penguins Run Swiftly
mnemonics.content: Step, Perceptron, ReLU, Sigmoid - four common activation functions in order: S(tep) P(erceptron) R(eLU) S(igmoid)

mnemonics.title: Weights Want Winners
mnemonics.content: Weights are adjusted based on error: New Weight = Old Weight + (Learning Rate × Error × Input). Remember: Weights want to WIN (Weight + Input Number) against error

mnemonics.title: BINA - Bias In Needs Adjustment
mnemonics.content: Bias (b) helps shift the decision boundary. Think: BINA shifts opinion - bias shifts the activation threshold

mnemonics.title: TATA - Think Activate Then Adjust
mnemonics.content: Think (compute weighted sum) → Activate (apply function) → Then Adjust (update weights if wrong). Sequence for perceptron learning

acronyms.expansion: Sum of (weight times input) plus bias
acronyms.usage: This is the weighted sum calculation - the core computation in the neuron body (cell body/soma)
Controls how much weights change during each update. Typical values: 0.01 to 0.1
Takes the weighted sum and produces the final output (0/1, -1/1, or probability)
The difference between expected output and actual output; used to calculate weight updates
Perceptron weight update rule - used in training to minimize error
A perceptron can implement NAND gate, meaning it can represent any Boolean function (universal approximator for logic)

visualTips.visualization: Draw a circle (neuron) with input lines (dendrites) coming from left, weights written on each line, a processing unit in middle (Σ), and output arrow (axon) going right. Like a factory receiving raw materials, processing, and sending finished product.
Imagine a straight line (2D) or plane (3D) cutting the input space. The perceptron outputs 1 on one side, 0 on other. Visualize it as a wall separating two teams - you're either on side A or side B.
Think of a shopping bill: each item (input) × its price (weight), summed up, plus additional fixed charges (bias). The total determines if you buy (activate) or not.

Imagine scatter plot points of two classes. Can you draw a single straight line to separate all red dots from all blue dots? If YES → linearly separable (perceptron can solve). If NO → need multi-layer networks.
Imagine adjusting a chef's recipe: if dish is too salty (error positive), reduce salt (weight). If too bland (error negative), add more. Learning rate = how much you adjust per iteration.

analogies.analogy: Think of a job interview: Your qualifications (inputs) are weighted by their importance (relevance to job). Your experience (weight 0.9) matters more than hobbies (weight 0.1). The HR adds a minimum cutoff (bias). You either get selected (1) or rejected (0) based on total score.

Weights are like the importance/strength of connections in your brain. Meeting a colleague frequently (high weight) strongly influences your opinion about the company, while偶尔 meeting (low weight) has less influence.

Bias is like your base expectation or mood. Even if nothing special happens today (inputs = 0), if you're in a good mood (positive bias), you might still be happy (activate). If depressed (negative bias), nothing makes you happy.

Activation function is like a bouncer at a club. The weighted sum is your 'score' based on outfit, confidence, friend list. The bouncer (activation) checks if you meet threshold → either enter (1) or not (0).

Learning rate is like how drastically you change your study strategy after a failed exam. High η = completely change textbook, tutor, schedule. Low η = just readjust a few chapters. Too high = unstable, too low = slow learning.

A single perceptron is like a simple person who can only draw ONE straight line to separate things. For XOR problem (both ON or both OFF = 1, mixed = 0), you need to bend the line. One straight line can't do this - need multiple perceptrons (network) like team meetings to solve complex decisions.
